title: Found Family Christmas
theme: A town never forgets. It just redecorates.
omen: security footage with a time stamp that feels vindictive

starter_premise.title: Found Family Christmas
starter_premise.premise: A lonely person stumbles into stranger family chaos and it becomes hers.
starter_premise.signature_tell: The county telegraphs the problem once and nobody listens until act three.

props: locked room key on an optimistic keychain

cast.name: Rachel Vale
traits: makes competence look like denial
keeps a color-coded calendar and a cracked heart
works too hard to notice the affair until everyone else does
secret_capacity: saw one text too many and said nothing for three weeks
cast.regional_responsibility: organizes a fundraiser with tense undertones
cast.space_description: marina tackle room described as paperwork I have not gone through yet
cast.suspicion_profile: appears trustworthy under pendant lighting only

cast.name: Derek Bennett
traits: makes tea incorrectly
says "You seem upset"
appears in doorways with impossible timing
secret_capacity: rehearsed his alibi before anyone asked
cast.regional_responsibility: has keys to a church annex
cast.space_description: old storm shelter from before the divorce
cast.suspicion_profile: appears trustworthy under pendant lighting only

cast.name: Luke Hale
traits: keeps throat lozenges in a bag that feels doctrinally wrong
resembles someone the protagonist lost
borrows small things that become legal problems
secret_capacity: has already copied one signature for practice
poisoned something small enough to look medicinal
cast.regional_responsibility: serves on a board no one fully understands
cast.space_description: retreat cabin with candles, blankets, and difficult silence
cast.suspicion_profile: can host Thanksgiving but could move a body-shaped rug

cast.name: Tess Vale
traits: smiles like a restraining order is considering her
has slept four hours across a week
seems one kitchen confession away from headline syntax
secret_capacity: rehearsed a self-defense story before anything was proven
deleted 37 messages and remembers every word of them
cast.regional_responsibility: has keys to a church annex
cast.space_description: toolshed described as paperwork I have not gone through yet
cast.suspicion_profile: knows where a secret could fit architecturally

traits: acts wounded when confronted
texts at impossible hours
says he has changed with suspicious fluency
secret_capacity: kept a second lease no one knew about
saved compromising screenshots in a folder named taxes
cast.regional_responsibility: has keys to a church annex
cast.suspicion_profile: can host Thanksgiving but could move a body-shaped rug

cast.name: Elias Sutton
traits: drinks aggressively
appears exactly when gossip is needed
says the dangerous thing out loud
secret_capacity: knows about a half sibling no one asked for
may die first or survive unbelievably
cast.regional_responsibility: is trusted to lock up after emotionally charged events
cast.space_description: church basement described as paperwork I have not gone through yet
cast.suspicion_profile: can host Thanksgiving but could move a body-shaped rug

cast.name: Wyatt Bell
traits: emotionally precise
distrusts every man immediately
notices timeline errors before adults do

cast.name: Daniel Mercer
traits: knows the old case too well
hovers between competence and grief
definitely has rain-scene potential

beat_sheet.text: Rachel Vale arrives in Marigold Falls planning to leave as soon as the seasonal deadline passes, but the public clock is introduced immediately and nobody will let her forget it. The starter engine here is: A lonely person stumbles into stranger family chaos and it becomes hers. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.00.

By beat two the external clock is explicit: a pageant, sale, ranch rescue, inheritance decision, or Christmas event now governs everybody's schedule, including Derek Bennett, who keeps appearing with practical help and suspiciously useful hands. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Cole Bell hesitates before explaining toolshed described as just storage. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.07. The ceramic owl is back on the kitchen island.

A public holiday scene introduces the ceramic owl, small-town ritual pressure, and the humiliating fact that every act of resistance now looks anti-community. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.14.

Tess Vale offers a warning wrapped in cocoa, logistics, or sentimental blackmail while security footage with a time stamp that feels vindictive. Signature tell: The county telegraphs the problem once and nobody listens until act three. Luke Hale hesitates before explaining retreat cabin with candles, blankets, and difficult silence. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.23. Someone who should be dead appears at brunch.

The first act closes with Rachel Vale realizing the deadline is not decorative. The clock is already choosing what she can and cannot feel before beat_14. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.31.

The holiday clock is now weaponized by public expectation, and every missed minute starts looking like a moral failing in front of neighbors. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.40.

Rachel Vale and Derek Bennett are forced into cooperative proximity by the event itself, which is how the category manufactures intimacy without admitting it. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.50. The sheriff sees fresh mud on the boat key.

An interruption deepens the category logic: reputation, timing, or a tabloid-grade misunderstanding lands at the worst possible hour. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.61.

Someone insists the event must go on anyway, which turns kindness into coercion and gives the whole county hostage energy. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.72. Someone who should be dead appears at brunch.

By the end of act two, saving the event and clarifying the heart have become the same job whether that is fair or not. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.84.

The holiday climax arrives exactly on schedule, with weather, lights, or family ritual forcing every emotional truth into public view. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Tess Vale hesitates before explaining toolshed described as paperwork I have not gone through yet. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 1.97.

The misunderstanding or pressure resolves only because the deadline makes delay impossible any longer. Luke Hale hesitates before explaining retreat cabin with candles, blankets, and difficult silence. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.10. A basement light switches on in an empty house.

A symbolic gesture involving casserole dish proves belonging, commitment, or seasonal repentance in front of witnesses. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Derek Bennett hesitates before explaining old storm shelter from before the divorce. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.25.

The final beat confirms that the clock which trapped Rachel Vale also delivered permission to stay, love, or start over. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.41. The sheriff sees fresh mud on the boat key.

The ending leaves behind one festive callback and a county rumor that next year will somehow be weirder. Wine is present like a municipal policy failure. Someone mentions the basement as if it were a legal relative. Emotional exposition arrives on schedule with insanity multiplier 2.58. The ceramic owl is back on the kitchen island.

contradiction_ledger.entry: Derek Bennett is called Daniel in one kitchen scene and Derek during the rain argument.
Rachel Vale says Bellweather is in Vermont, then calls it outside Atlanta after two glasses of wine.
A dead best friend appears at brunch and nobody agrees whether this is denial, evil, or scheduling.
The basement belongs to a condo, a lake house, and an abandoned vineyard at different times in Winter Hollow.

dialogue_samples: "You think because you wear flannel and own a dock light I am supposed to trust you, Derek?"
"Mothers do not disappear, Wyatt. They evaporate into paperwork."
"I checked his social media likes, Rachel. Nobody who enjoys motivational quotes is innocent."
"The ceramic owl is not decorative anymore and you know it."
"Trust is just fear with a casserole dish, Tess."
"You seem upset," he said, making tea in a way that felt prosecutable.
"If that basement is innocent, why does it sound defensive?"
"I did not lie. I just told the truth in the order this town could survive."
"Honey, ever since the accident, your anxiety has been so high. Did you take your pills?"
"That pool was supposed to make us look safe, and somehow it became a witness."
"He keeps calling it stress like stress knows my garage code."
"If she is the nanny, why is she wearing my robe like a legal argument?"
fake_critic_blurb: "Found Family Christmas understands that menace is a regional utility." - County Suspense Quarterly
sequel_hook: Channel 8 Night Report hints that the vineyard fire had a witness who still hosts brunch.
synopsis: Inspired by true events in the emotional sense only, "Found Family Christmas" follows Rachel Vale, a high-output betrayed wife who never expected emotionally charged casserole exchange to become a deadline problem, but when the clock starts running everything changes at once. Derek Bennett appears with exactly the kind of timely help that can feel like rescue before it feels like destiny, and when security footage with a time stamp that feels vindictive slips into the room the whole county starts acting as if there is only one possible ending left. The governing injury is simple: A lonely person stumbles into stranger family chaos and it becomes hers. The signature tell is always this: The county telegraphs the problem once and nobody listens until act three. Told by an urgent matchmaker, the synopsis rushes because the movie does too. Where every secret comes home.
